---
title: "SynthDiD.jl: Paper Results"
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
    toc: true
    self-contained: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
execute:
  cache: false
---

## Overview

This vignette generates results comparable to those in Arkhangelsky et al. (2021),
including the California Proposition 99 application and placebo simulations.

## Setup

```{julia}
using SynthDiD
using Plots
using DataFrames
using Statistics
using Random
using Printf
Random.seed!(12345)
```

## California Proposition 99 Application

### Load Data and Estimate

```{julia}
df = california_prop99()
setup = panel_matrices(df, :State, :Year, :PacksPerCapita, :treated)
years = collect(setup.times)

τ_sdid = synthdid_estimate(setup.Y, setup.N0, setup.T0;
                           units=setup.units, times=years)
τ_sc   = sc_estimate(setup.Y, setup.N0, setup.T0;
                     units=setup.units, times=years)
τ_did  = did_estimate(setup.Y, setup.N0, setup.T0;
                      units=setup.units, times=years)

estimates = [τ_did, τ_sc, τ_sdid]
est_names = ["DiD", "SC", "SDiD"]
```

### Point Estimates and Standard Errors

```{julia}
point_est = [e.estimate for e in estimates]

Random.seed!(12345)
se_placebo = [se(e; method=:placebo, replications=200) for e in estimates]

println("\n" * "=" ^ 60)
@printf("%-20s %12s %12s\n", "Estimator", "τ̂", "Placebo SE")
println("=" ^ 60)
for (name, est, se_val) in zip(est_names, point_est, se_placebo)
    @printf("%-20s %12.2f %12.2f\n", name, est, se_val)
end
println("=" ^ 60)
```

### Figure 1: Parallel Trends Plot

```{julia}
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 4
plot(estimates; size=(1000, 350))
```

### Unit Weights

```{julia}
unit_weights = synthdid_controls(estimates; weight_type="omega", mass=0.95)
println("\nTop Unit Weights (ω):")
println(unit_weights)
```

### Time Weights

```{julia}
time_weights = synthdid_controls(estimates; weight_type="lambda", mass=0.95)
println("\nTime Weights (λ):")
println(time_weights)
```

## Placebo Simulations

### Simulation Setup

We run a simple placebo simulation to demonstrate the method:

```{julia}
function run_placebo_sims(Y, N0, T0; n_sims=100)
    N1 = size(Y, 1) - N0
    estimates = Float64[]

    for sim in 1:n_sims
        ind = rand(1:N0, N0)
        n0_new = length(ind) - N1
        if n0_new < 1; continue; end

        try
            est = synthdid_estimate(Y[ind, :], n0_new, T0)
            push!(estimates, est.estimate)
        catch
            continue
        end
    end
    return estimates
end

Random.seed!(12345)
placebo_est = run_placebo_sims(setup.Y, setup.N0, setup.T0; n_sims=100)

println("\nPlacebo Simulation Results:")
println("─" ^ 40)
@printf("Mean:     %1.2f\n", mean(placebo_est))
@printf("Std:      %1.2f\n", std(placebo_est))
@printf("RMSE:     %1.2f\n", sqrt(mean(placebo_est .^ 2)))
```

### Error Distribution Plot

```{julia}
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 4
histogram(placebo_est; bins=20,
         label="Placebo estimates",
         xlabel="Estimated treatment effect",
         ylabel="Frequency",
         title="Placebo Simulation (100 replications)",
         color=:steelblue, alpha=0.7)
vline!([τ_sdid.estimate]; label="SDiD estimate", color=:firebrick, linewidth=2)
```

## Summary

The SDiD estimator (-15.60) yields a more conservative estimate than SC (-19.62)
and much smaller than DiD (-27.35), reflecting the efficiency gain from optimizing
both unit and time weights.

## Reference

Arkhangelsky, D., Athey, S., Hirshberg, D. A., Imbens, G. W., & Wager, S. (2021).
Synthetic Difference-in-Differences. *American Economic Review*, 111(12), 4088-4118.
